subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods & Experimental Design
stimulus:
Professor Gonzalez hypothesizes that students will remember more information from his detailed course
description if it is printed on yellow paper than if it is printed on standard white paper. To test this hypothesis,
he put the names of all his students into a bowl, then drew out names to assign them to two different groups. He
gave one group of the students in his class a course description printed on white paper, and gave the other
group of students a course description printed on yellow paper. Students were instructed to thoroughly read the
description outside of class in preparation for their next class. In the next class, he gave all students a
ten-question quiz asking them about the information found on the course description. Professor Gonzalez then
compared the number of correct answers for each group of students. The statistically significant results are
depicted in the graph.

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of the dependent variable.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Response must indicate that the dependent variable is operationally defined as the student’s score on the course description quiz.
Acceptable: The dependent variable is operationally defined as the score on the course description; The dependent variable is the quiz score/grade on the quiz.
1 pt: Response must indicate that the experimental group is the group with the course description printed on yellow paper.
Acceptable: The experimental group is the people who have the yellow paper.

parts.prompt: Explain how In addition to the manipulation of an independent variable, identify the procedure Professor Gonzalez used to make this study a true experiment. relates to the scenario.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Professor Gonzalez used to make this study a true experiment. Response must indicate that the procedure used to make this a true experiment is random assignment.
Acceptable: Professor Gonzalez randomly assigned the participants to groups.; Professor Gonzalez put people in groups randomly.

parts.prompt: Explain how the data as presented in the graph do or do not support Professor Gonzalez’s hypothesis.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: hypothesis. Response must indicate that data support the hypothesis because the experimental group (i.e., the yellow paper group) scored higher on the test.
Acceptable: Professor Gonzalez thought that the experimental group would score higher, and they; The bar for the yellow paper group is higher than the bar for the white paper group,

parts.prompt: Explain how Context-dependent memory might relate to the scenario.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Response must indicate that the student’s memory retrieval is affected if some external element (e.g., room, sensory cues, equipment) that is present when the information was learned/encoded is present/the same or absent/not the same as when they take a quiz.
Acceptable: If students learn the information in a particular classroom, they should take a quiz in; If a student chews gum while studying, they should chew gum while taking a quiz.

parts.prompt: Explain how Yerkes-Dodson law might relate to the scenario.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Response must indicate that students would perform best under a level of optimal arousal. OR Response must indicate that students would perform worse if they were under- or over- aroused. Synonyms for “under-aroused” can include a reference to the amount (e.g., too little, under, insufficient). Synonyms for “over-aroused” can include a reference to the amount (e.g., too much, overly, super).
Acceptable: Students might be too aroused on the quiz, so they don’t do well.; Students may be at their optimal arousal, so they end up doing well on the quiz.

parts.prompt: Explain how Low level of the Big Five trait of conscientiousness might relate to the scenario.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Response must indicate that a low level of an enduring characteristic of conscientiousness would hinder student performance on the quiz (e.g., not being diligent, being less hardworking, being unorganized, being undisciplined, being irresponsible, being unprepared, etc.).
Acceptable: Students who aren’t hardworking may be hindered from doing well on the quiz.
